coredata.dc:title: Enhancing photoelectrochemical water oxidation activity of BiVO4 photoanode through the Co-catalytic effect of Ni(OH)2 and carbon quantum dots

Xia, Lixin
coredata.dc:description: The CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode was fabricated by introducing CQDs with fluorescence and optical properties. The CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode exhibited a low water oxidation onset potential (∼0.2 V vs. RHE) and a photocurrent density of 3.05 mA cm−2 at a voltage of 1.23 V vs. RHE, which is about 2.5 times than that of the unmodified BiVO4 electrode, and the charge injection efficiency is 65%. Surface dynamics test showed that CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode had higher charge transfer rate and lower charge recombination rate than BiVO4 electrode. As a cheap and stable catalyst for water oxidation, Ni(OH)2 combined with CQDs can effectively improve the migration of photogenerated electrons and holes, thereby improving the photoelectrochemical (PEC) water oxidation activity of the photoanode.

head.ce:title: Enhancing photoelectrochemical water oxidation activity of BiVO4 photoanode through the Co-catalytic effect of Ni(OH)2 and carbon quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode was fabricated by introducing CQDs with fluorescence and optical properties. The CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode exhibited a low water oxidation onset potential (∼0.2 V vs. RHE) and a photocurrent density of 3.05 mA cm−2 at a voltage of 1.23 V vs. RHE, which is about 2.5 times than that of the unmodified BiVO4 electrode, and the charge injection efficiency is 65%. Surface dynamics test showed that CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode had higher charge transfer rate and lower charge recombination rate than BiVO4 electrode. As a cheap and stable catalyst for water oxidation, Ni(OH)2 combined with CQDs can effectively improve the migration of photogenerated electrons and holes, thereby improving the photoelectrochemical (PEC) water oxidation activity of the photoanode.

ce:section.ce:para: It is one of the ideal ways to convert solar energy into chemical energy to solve energy and environmental problems by using solar energy to split water to produce hydrogen by simulating photosynthesis system. A well-designed active photoanode for photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting is critical for future sustainable energy conversion. It usually requires an effective combination of catalyst and photoanode to improve surface dynamics and suppress surface recombination [1–4]. In recent years, many semiconductor materials of metal oxides have been widely used as photoanode materials. BiVO4 has been proved to be one of the most promising photoanode materials for PEC water oxidation reaction due to its suitable band gap, good light absorption and low synthesis cost [5–7]. However, the photoelectric response performance of BiVO4 electrode is restricted by strong electron-hole recombination, weak water oxidation kinetics, and poor chemical stability under strong alkaline conditions [8]. Therefore, it is necessary to modify the BiVO4 electrode to improve water oxidation performance of PEC.

So far, various strategies have been explored to improve the PEC water oxidation activity of BiVO4 photoanode. For instance, doping with heteroatoms and introducing vacancies to enhance the conductivity of BiVO4. The preparation of BiVO4 with twin structure [9], the reduction of defects [10] and the control of crystal orientation [6,11] can improve charge transport performance and surface reactivity. In addition, it has been reported that suitable hole transport layers [12], electron transport layers [13,14], surface passivation layers [15], and interface charge mediators [16] can efficiently suppress the interfacial recombination of accumulated holes with electrons. It is worth mentioning that the electrode modified by water oxidation catalyst can promote the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and improve the water oxidation ability of PEC water splitting reaction [17–22]. Many oxygen evolution catalysts (OECs), such as Co3O4 [23], CoP [24–26], CoF2 [27], FeOOH [28,29], cobalt phosphate (CoPi) [30–32], cobalt phosphate (CoBi) [33,34], ferric phosphate (FePi) [35], CoFe Prussian blue [36], and NiCo-layered double hydroxide [37] have been coated on BiVO4 photoanodes, which can improve the surface PEC water oxidation kinetics, thereby boosting the PEC performance of the BiVO4 photoanode. Although various OECs have made great progress as co-catalysts, the photocurrent density of co-catalyst modified BiVO4 photoanode is still below its theoretical limit, so it is still a great challenge to find a new co-catalyst that is easy to prepare and conducive to water oxidation.

In recent years, carbon-based materials have also been widely studied as photocatalysts for solar water splitting [38,39]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can enhance the photocatalytic water oxidation activity of TiO2, BiVO4 and g-C3N4 semiconductors [40–44]. Kang et al. believed that the favorable photocatalytic activity of the designed NiO/CDs/BiVO4 composites was attributed to the addition of CDs, which largely inhibited the recombination rate of electrons and holes [45]. Zhou et al. designed an excellent CQDs/FeOOH/BiVO4 photoanode to improve the performance of PEC, and pointed out that CQDs significantly boosted the light harvesting in the ultraviolet and visible regions [46]. As a highly active catalyst, CQDS can decompose water through a two-electron route, which is superior to the one-step four electron route in kinetics [17,44,47–51]. Therefore, we prepared CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 or Ni(OH)2/CQDs/BiVO4 composite photoanode by growing CQDs and Ni(OH)2 hollow spheres on the nanoporous BiVO4 electrode by a solvothermal method (Scheme 1 ). Under the irradiation of simulated sunlight (AM 1.5 G, 100 mW cm−2), the CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode has a lower onset potential of water oxidation and a photocurrent density of 3.05 mA cm−2 (1.23 V vs. RHE), which exhibited better PEC performance and excellent water oxidation stability than CQDs/BiVO4 and Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanodes. In addition, kinetic studies showed that the CQDs-loaded Ni(OH)2 catalyst could effectively inhibit surface recombination and promote hole injection into the electrolyte with a charge injection efficiency of 65% (1.23 V vs. RHE). As a low-cost non-precious metal catalyst for water oxidation, Ni(OH)2 provides important significance for the design and development of efficient and reasonable photoanode.

BiVO4 electrode was prepared by the electrodeposition method [52]. First, 150 mL deionized water was added into a clean beaker, and concentrated nitric acid was added while stirring to adjust the pH to 1.7, KI (0.4 M) was dissolved in the above solution. After stirring for 5 min, 2.91 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was added to the solution and the mixture was ultrasonicated for 20 min. The above solution was mixed with anhydrous ethanol solution containing 0.23 M p-benzoquinone and stirred for 20 min. In the three-electrode device, clean FTO is used as the working electrode for electrodeposition, and the constant potential of −0.1 V versus Ag/AgCl for 5 min to obtain the BiOI film. It was then rinsed with deionized water and dried with N2, and add the solution containing vanadyl acetylacetonate (VO(acac)2) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) to the BiOI film. Afterward, the electrode was heated for 2 h at 450 °C in a muffle furnace. Then it was naturally cooled to room temperature, and all the electrodes were taken out and immersed in 1.0 M NaOH solution. The excess V2O5 on the BiVO4 electrode was removed after 30 min. Finally, the electrodes were washed with deionized water and blow-dried with N2.

According to the method reported in the literature [53], citric acid was selected as the carbon source. 2.0 g citric acid was added to a 50 mL clean beaker and heated to 180 °C for 5 h to form a high viscosity liquid. After the beaker was cooled at room temperature, add 5 mL of deionized water to make liquid dissolved. The product was collected by centrifugation, dialysis with deionized water for 24 h to remove small molecular impurities. The purified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) solution was freeze-dried to obtain a brown solid.

The Ni(OH)2 was synthesized according to the method reported in literature [54]. In this synthesis process, 0.58 g of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved in 40 mL of anhydrous ethanol and stirred for several minutes to form a green clarification solution. Then, 4.0 mL of oleamine and 20 mL of anhydrous ethanol were successively added into the above solution. After stirring for 30 min, the mixture solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated at 180 °C. Finally, the precipitates were collected by centrifugation, washed with cyclohexane and anhydrous ethanol, and dried in vacuum.

Dissolve 50 mg of Ni(OH)2 in 20 mL of anhydrous ethanol, add 3 mL of 20 mg/mL of CQDS solution, and then ultrasonicated for 20 min. The solution was then transferred to a 10 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, in which the BiVO4 electrode was tilted and heated 10 h at 80 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the electrodes were taken out and rinsed with deionized water, obtaining CQDS + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode.

Add 3 mL of 20 mg/mL CQDS solution into 20 mL of absolute ethanol. The mixed solution was transferred to a 10 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, and BiVO4 electrode was tilted into the reactor. The CQDs/BiVO4 photoanode was obtained after being heated 10 h at 80 °C, cooled at room temperature, and rinsed with deionized water.

50 mg Ni(OH)2 was dissolved in 20 mL anhydrous ethanol and ultrasonicated for 20 min. The solution was then transferred to a 10 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave in which the BiVO4 electrode was tilted. The Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode was obtained after being heated 10 h at 80 °C, cooled at room temperature, and then rinsed with deionized water.

Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO)-coated glass slides were purchased from Zhuhai Kaivo Electronic Components Co., Ltd. Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and Bi(NO3)3·5H2O were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). p-benzoquinone was purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China). Oleyl amine and citric acid were purchased from Energy Chemical. Cyclohexane, DMSO and all other reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The organic solvents were AR grade and used without further purification.

The morphologies of the CQDs and photoanodes (BiVO4, CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and Ni(OH)2/BiVO4) were investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images and energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum using a Quanta SU8000 scanning electron microscope instrument operated at 10 kV. The crystalline phases of Ni(OH)2 power and the electrodes were characterized by XRD (powder X-ray diffraction) on a Bruker Smart APEX II powder diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image was obtained using a JEOL 2100 operated at 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was tested using a K-Alpha XPS spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). Raman spectra were obtained by using a Raman spectrometer (Renishaw in Via) with a laser excitation wavelength of 633 nm. Fourier-transform-infrared (FT-IR) measurements were performed by a spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Spectrum One, USA). The UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra were obtained by a UV–visible spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer Lambda 35; Norwalk, CT, USA). The room-temperature photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using a luminescence spectrometer (HITACHI, F-2000, Japan). The content of elements was tested by inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP, PE- 43008300).

The PEC performance of electrodes was measured by a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instrument, Shanghai, China). A three-electrode system was constructed with a platinum wire as the counter electrode, the glass carbon (GC) electrode as the working electrode and an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) and Cyclic voltammetry (CV) curve were conducted in a 0.1 M KOH solution (pH 13.0).

The PEC performance of photoanodes was measured by a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instrument, Shanghai, China) under irradiation. A three-electrode device consisted of a platinum wire as the counter electrode, the BiVO4, CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanodes (active area = 1.0 cm2) as the working electrodes, and an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode. The photoanode was irradiated with a 300 W xenon lamp (Beijing, CEL-HXF300C) that simulates solar light (AM 1.5G), and the light intensity was 100 mW cm−2. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) with a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 was conducted in a pH 8.6 0.2 M boric acid buffer solution. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out with an ac voltage amplitude of 5 mV at 1.23 V versus RHE, frequency range from 10 kHz to 0.01 Hz under solar light irradiation.

IMPS measurements were measured by using a Zahner IMPS electrochemical workstation (Zahner electrochemical company, Germany, IM6ex) in a three-electrode system. Modulated illumination was supplied with a light-emitting diode (LED, 420 nm, 10 W m−2) that permitted superposition of sinusoidal modulation (∼10%) on a dc illumination level. The photocurrent was recorded as a function of frequency (from 10 kHz to 100 mHz) under illumination at different potentials.

The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in this experiment were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images in Fig. 1 a illustrate the shape, size and crystalline structure of the synthesized CQDs. It can be seen from the figure that the size of CQDs is small, and the shape is a scattered and relatively uniform sphere. The particle size distribution of CQDs is 1.6–3.4 nm, with an average particle size of 2.6 nm, and the distribution is relatively uniform (Fig. S1). The lattice spacing of CQDs is 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of the graphite region. As carbon nanomaterials, the particle size of CQDs is generally less than 10 nm [55]. TEM and HRTEM images show that the synthesized CQDs meet the size requirements of carbon nanomaterials with good dispersion without agglomeration.

To further prove the structure and properties of the synthesized CQDs, the nanomaterial CQDs were characterized by Raman spectroscopy, infrared absorption (FT-IR) spectroscopy, and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 1b-d. The Raman spectrum of CQDs is shown in Fig. 1b. The peak at 1580 cm−1 (G band) corresponds to the E2g mode of graphite, which is related to the in-plane vibration of the sp2-bonded carbon atom in a two-dimensional (2D) hexagonal lattice. The D band around 1360 cm−1 is associated with carbon atom defects of sp3 bond in the termination surface of disordered graphite or glassy carbon. According to the infrared absorption spectrum of CQDs in Fig. 1c, it can be seen that there are a large number of carbonyl, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CQDs. The light-response characteristics of CQDs were studied by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1d, CQDs have a certain photoresponse in the UV–vis wavelength range [56–60]. Therefore, although CQDs as a photosensitizer are helpful in trapping visible solar photons and separating electron-hole pairs, the effect of BiVO4 enhancing the visible-light response is extremely weak.

The XRD pattern in Fig. S2 shows four diffraction peaks corresponding to the (003), (006), (101) and (110) planes of α-Ni(OH)2 single crystal phase (JCPDS 38–0715) [61–64], and no other impurity peaks were observed, indicating that the sample has a high purity. The XRD patterns of the FTO, BiVO4, CQDs, CQDs/BiVO4, Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 and CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanodes are presented in Fig. S3, from which we can see that characteristic peaks of BiVO4 can be observed at 18.68° (101), 19.00° (011), 28.97° (112), 30.55° (004), 46.75° (204) and 47.31° (024). CQDs show a weak characteristic peak of amorphous carbon, which is caused by the small size and low loading of CQDs. Since the load of co-catalyst Ni(OH)2 is also very small, no obvious characteristic peak of Ni(OH)2 can be seen in Fig. S3b. According to the test results of inductively coupled plasma emission spectroscopy (ICP), it is calculated that the amount of Ni(OH)2 catalyst loaded on the Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 and CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanodes is about 81.7 nmol cm−2 and 78.3 nmol cm−2, respectively.

The morphological characteristics of as-prepared samples were investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Fig. 2 shows the SEM images of Ni(OH)2 powder, BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4, CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4, and Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode (Fig. S4). As shown in Fig. 2 (a), Ni(OH)2 appears as a sphere of ∼2 μm, which is agglomerated by nanosheets. BiVO4 consists of uniformly sized nanoparticles with a nanoporous structure (Fig. 2b). The size of the CQDs is too small to be observed (Fig. 2c). Therefore, after the modification of CQDs and Ni(OH)2, as shown in Fig. 2d, only spherical Ni(OH)2 can be seen on the surface of BiVO4 electrode. The zeta potential of BiVO4 and Ni(OH)2 was about −2.0 mV and 3.2 mV respectively (Fig. S5). The positive and negative zeta potential indicates that BiVO4 and Ni(OH)2 can be combined together, and the system is relatively stable. To further obtain the typical structure and morphology of the samples, we conducted TEM and the corresponding HRTEM test. Fig. 2f and g clearly show that CQDs are adsorbed on the Ni(OH)2 nanosheets and loaded onto the surface of BiVO4. The lattice spacing of the CQDs and BiVO4 are 0.22 and 0.308 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2g and h. Ni(OH)2 is hollow spheres formed by the agglomeration of extremely thin layered structures (Fig. 2i). The energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum in Fig. 2e shows that Ni(OH)2 is distributed on the surface of BiVO4, and C, O, Ni, Bi, and V elements are also detected, confirming that CQDs and Ni(OH)2 were successfully modified onto the BiVO4 electrode.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted to analyze the detailed elemental composition and surface chemical state of the CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode. XPS confirmed the presence of Bi, V, O, Ni and C elements (Fig. 3 and Fig. S6). The high-resolution peaks of Bi 4f, V 2p, O 1s and Ni 2p for BiVO4,CQDs/BiVO4,Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 and CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanodes are observed in Fig. 3. The binding energies of BiVO4 at 158.7 and 164.1 eV are attributed to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 of Bi3+, respectively (Fig. 3a). The signals of V 2p1/2 and V 2p3/2 are located at 524.5 and 516.6 eV, respectively (Fig. 3b), indicating that V species are in the state of V5+ [65]. For CQDs/BiVO4, the binding energies of the Bi 4f and V 2p did not change, indicating that no changes in the valence states of Bi and V were observed after CQDs modification. In contrast, the Bi 4f and V 2p peaks of the Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 and CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 shifted to lower binding energies compared with BiVO4, indicating electron transfer from Ni(OH)2 to BiVO4 increase the electron cloud density around Bi and V [66–68]. In Fig. 3c, the O 1s XPS spectra of four samples show asymmetric peaks, which can be roughly split into three peaks, corresponding to lattice oxygen, the lattice hydroxyl group and dissociation or chemisorption of oxygen species, respectively [69–71]. Dissociation or chemisorption of oxygen is caused by surface chemisorption such as hydroxyl and H2O [72,73]. The O 1s XPS spectrum of BiVO4 displays main lattice O at 529.7 eV with O of surface absorbed –OH at 531.4 eV. While O1s spectra for CQDs/BiVO4, Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 and CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 were divided into three peaks at 529.7, 531.4 and 532.9 eV, which could be assigned the lattice O (Bi–O, Ni–O), lattice hydroxyl group (Bi–OH, Ni–OH) and the O in adsorbed water or the adsorbed hydroxyl group (OH−) during hydrolysis process [74–76]. Fig. 3d shows the similarity of Ni 2p core-level spectra for Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 and CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4, indicating the presence of the same Ni species state in the samples. The Ni 2p characteristic peaks at 855.1 and 872.6 eV correspond to the Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 [51,77], and the binding energies at 861.2 and 878.9 eV are attributed to the satellite peak. Ni species mainly exist in two oxidation states of +2 and + 3, and are the main active species for hole transfer and water oxidation [64]. Meanwhile, the binding energy of Ni 2p in CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 shift to a higher value compared with that of Ni(OH)2/BiVO4, showing an elevated oxidation state of Ni, further demonstrating that the introduction of CQDs facilitates the electrons transfer from Ni(OH)2 to BiVO4 [51,60,78]. Meanwhile, this is also evidenced by the shift of the Ni 2p peak in Fig. S7. In addition, we also performed fluorescence tests on samples of CQDs and CQDs mixed with Ni(OH)2, respectively (Fig. S8). When the excitation wavelength was 380 nm, CQDs had obvious fluorescence, but CQDs + Ni(OH)2 produced fluorescence quenching, indicating the interaction between Ni(OH)2 and CQDs. In Fig. S6, there is no change in the binding energies of C 1s, indicating that the loading of Ni(OH)2 had no effect on the chemical state of C element.

Since Ni(OH)2 has been extensively studied as an electrocatalyst, we first tested the electrochemical activity of different electrodes in an oxidation systems. We used the catalyst-loaded glassy carbon electrode as the working electrode to record the OER polarization curve in a 1.0 M KOH solution. Fig. S9 illustrates that the CQDs + Ni(OH)2/GC electrode shows the largest current and the lowest initial potential compared with the pure CQDs electrode and the Ni(OH)2 electrode. This means that CQDs plays an irreplaceable role in significantly enhancing the catalytic activity of CQDs + Ni(OH)2/GC electrode for water oxidation. We analyze that CQDs + Ni(OH)2/GC electrode as an electrocatalytic material with high water oxidation activity in alkaline solution, which was mainly attributed to the catalytic effect of Ni(OH)2. For Ni(OH)2/GC electrode, the oxidation peaks for Ni2+ to Ni3+/Ni4+ clearly appeared around 1.45 V vs. RHE. This can be seen from some references [54,79,80]. While for CQDs + Ni(OH)2/GC electrode, the oxidation peak site showed an obvious negative shift, indicating that CQDs promote charge transfer and improve the electrocatalytic performance [78].

Based on the above results, we compared the PEC properties of the as-prepared CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4, Ni(OH)2/BiVO4, and CQDs/BiVO4 photoanodes. The PEC test was carried out in 0.2 M boric acid buffer solution using a typical three-electrode system. According to the LSV curves of the photoanodes in Fig. 4 a, we can see that all the modified photoanodes exhibit higher water oxidation activity than the unmodified BiVO4 electrode. In the dark condition, the photocurrent densities of all photoanodes is 0 (Fig. S10). Under the irradiation of simulated sunlight (AM 1.5 G, 100 mW cm−2), as shown in Fig. 4b, the photocurrent density of CQDs/BiVO4 is 1.71 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, and the photocurrent density of Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode is 1.94 mA cm−2. The photocurrent enhancement of CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode is the most obvious (3.05 mA cm−2), which is about 2.5 times that of the unmodified BiVO4 electrode. The remarkably increased photocurrent and decreased water oxidation onset potential (∼0.2 V vs. RHE) indicate that Ni(OH)2 is an efficient OER catalyst and can effectively improve the PEC performance of the photoanode. Interestingly, the introduction of CQDs has a significant impact on the system. We compared CQDs interacting with Ni(OH)2-modified the BiVO4 electrode and Ni(OH)2-modified the CQDs/BiVO4 photoanode, namely CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 and Ni(OH)2/CQDs/BiVO4. The results show that the photocurrent density of Ni(OH)2/CQDs/BiVO4 photoanode is about 2.63 mA cm−2 (1.23 V vs. RHE), and the catalytic activity is much lower than that of CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode. Moreover, the initial potential of Ni(OH)2/CQDs/BiVO4 is higher than that of CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4, indicating that the CQDs interact with Ni(OH)2 first and then modify on the BiVO4 electrode, which is more conducive to promoting the water oxidation reaction. In order to better analyze the effect of the addition of CQDs on the system, we also tested the hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity of each electrode. It can be seen from Fig. S11 that the contact angle θ of the BiVO4 electrode is very small and the hydrophilicity is very good. After doping CQDs, the θ angle is almost 0 and the material is completely wetted. When modified with Ni(OH)2, the hydrophobicity of the electrode is enhanced (the contact angle of Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 is about 93.5°). While the contact angle of CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 is about 23.1°, and the hydrophilicity is between BiVO4 and Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanodes. This result fully demonstrates that the introduction of CQDs increases the hydrophilicity of the electrode surface, gives full play to the catalytic effect of Ni(OH)2 and improves the PEC properties.

where J is the photocurrent density at the potential of Vbias, and Pin is the simulated solar light intensity. All measured potentials were transformed into RHE (ERHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.059 V × pH + 0.197 V). As shown in Fig. 4c, the maximum ABPE for the CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode is calculated to be 0.90% at 0.73 V vs. RHE, which is approximately 2.8 times that of the CQDs/BiVO4 photoanode (0.32% at 0.88 V vs. RHE), 2.1 times that of the Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode (0.44% at 0.84 V vs. RHE), and 4.5 times that of the bare BiVO4 electrode (0.20% at 0.91 V vs. RHE). The transient photocurrent curves of BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4, Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 and CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanodes are shown in Fig. S12, where the light on/off period is maintained for 10 s. The unmodified BiVO4 showed the weakest photoresponse under visible light irradiation, while the CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode showed the strongest light response. When the lamp was turned on, the CQDs/BiVO4 exhibited the most obvious characteristic spike compared with other photoanodes, followed by a slow decay, because photogenerated holes were excited into the electrode and accumulated at the electrode/electrolyte interface without injecting the electrolyte [81]. When the lamp was turned off, we observe a spike in the cathodic current due to the recombination of holes on the electrode surface with photogenerated electrons, indicating that CQDs do not significantly improve the surface dynamics of the photoanode. In contrast, there is less charge accumulation (smaller peak value) on the surface of Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 and CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanodes. This suggested that the Ni(OH)2 OECs could act as a hole storage layer, which tended to suppress charge recombination at the electrode/electrolyte interface and promote hole transport [82]. Meanwhile, the dark current density curves measured under dark conditions showed that the CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode had a much lower overpotential of OER and a higher current density compared with the pristine BiVO4 electrode (Fig. S10). Particularly, the onset potential of the CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 was 1.5 V vs. RHE, which was much lower than that of the other three electrodes (>1.7 V vs. RHE).

To further reveal the reason for the significant increase in photocurrent of BiVO4 electrode after co-modification of CQDs and Ni(OH)2, an easily oxidizable hole scavenger sodium sulfate (Na2SO3) was introduced to eliminate the hole injection barrier. The PEC water oxidation was performed in the 0.2 M boric acid buffer solution mixed with 1 M Na2SO3 (Fig. S13). For the BiVO4 electrode, we observed that a significant increase in photocurrent and a significant decrease in onset potential in the presence of Na2SO3, indicating that Na2SO3 eliminated surface recombination and facilitated the injection of holes into the electrolyte. Different from BiVO4 electrode, the photocurrent increase of CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode is smaller than that of the BiVO4 electrode due to the addition of Na2SO3, and the initial potential of water oxidation was almost the same in the presence and absence of Na2SO3. This suggests that Ni(OH)2 and CQDs jointly promote the charge transfer of BiVO4 to the electrode/electrolyte interface at low potential without Na2SO3. In addition, the photocurrent generated by CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode is slightly higher than that of almost 100% charge injection efficiency of the BiVO4 electrode in the presence of Na2SO3. This indicates that Ni(OH)2 and CQDs increase the rate of water oxidation in the whole potential range due to the effective hole injection at the electrode/electrolyte interface. For the convenience of quantitative discussion, the surface charge injection efficiency of water oxidation reaction can be obtained by the formula:
(2)
η
c h a r g e i n j e c t i o n
=
J
H 2
O
/
J
N
a 2
S
O 3
Where ηcharge injection is the charge injection yield to the electrolyte, JH2O and JNa2SO3 represent the photocurrent densities for the water oxidation of H2O and Na2SO3, respectively (more details are provided in Supporting Information). As shown in Fig. 4d, the surface charge injection efficiencies of BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanodes all remained below 40%, indicating that half of the holes were consumed by surface recombination rather than water oxidation reaction. In contrast, the charge injection of CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode was higher than that of CQDs/BiVO4 and Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanodes at all bias voltages, from 32.5% at 0.4 V to 60.9% at 1.0 V, and even to nearly 65.7% at 1.23 V vs. RHE, indicating that the joint effect of CQDs and Ni(OH)2 restrains the charge recombination on the electrode surface. As shown in Fig. S14, the charge separation efficiency of CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 reaches about 68% at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which is about 10% higher than that of the BiVO4 photoanode. When CQDs are modified, the charge separation efficiency of CQDs/BiVO4 photoanodes is improved to some extent, but this improvement is limited. In contrast, the current density of Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode is almost the same as that of the BiVO4 photoanode, which does not improve the charge separation efficiency. It shows that Ni(OH)2 only improves the surface charge injection. Based on the above results, it can be deduced that the increase in current density of the CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode is mainly due to the improved migration of photogenerated electrons and holes in the Ni(OH)2 combined CQDs.

The co-modification of CQDs and Ni(OH)2 can accelerate the transfer of the holes generated by BiVO4 to the electrode/electrolyte interface and participate in the water oxidation reaction, which is further confirmed by the fitting results of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (Fig. 4e). It can be seen from the figure that the impedance radius of each electrode from large to small is BiVO4 > CQDs/BiVO4 > Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 > CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4. The largest radius of the BiVO4 electrode indicates a higher interfacial charge-transfer barrier. The CQDs/BiVO4 and Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanodes have lower charge-transfer resistance than the BiVO4 electrode. The lowest charge-transfer resistance of CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode illustrates faster charge transfer kinetics at the photoanode/electrolyte interface during water oxidation. The EIS results suggested that CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode exhibited increased interfacial charge transfer and lower resistance through the co-modification of CQDs and Ni(OH)2, which correlated with its improved charge injection and higher photocurrent.

The incident photon-to-current efficiency (IPCE) of each wavelength was tested by irradiation with a 300 W xenon lamp with an AM 1.5G filter. The IPCE value was calculated by the equation:
(3)
I P C E
( % )
=
1240 × J
λ ×
P
i n
× 100 %
where: J is the photocurrent density at a specific wavelength, Pin is the measured light power density, and λ is the wavelength of incident light. The wavelength dependence of the IPCEs for the BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4, Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 and CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanodes were examined at 1.23 V vs. RHE (Fig. 4f). In the wavelength range of 350–465 nm, the IPCE of BiVO4 electrode is about 16%. CQDs played an active role as photocatalyst, and the IPCE of CQDs/BiVO4 increased by about 6.5%. The IPCE values of Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 are very close to those of CQDs. However, when CQDs and Ni(OH)2 were simultaneously loaded, the PEC performance of the BiVO4 electrode was significantly improved. The IPCE of CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode reached about 51% (350–465 nm). This was also verified by UV–vis absorption spectra of different photoanodes. The spectra in Fig. S15 showed that the loading of CQDs could enhance the light absorption of the BiVO4 electrode to a certain extent, while Ni(OH)2 could hardly improve the light absorption capacity. The above results indicate that the combined effect of CQDs and Ni(OH)2 can enhance the charge injection efficiency and certain light-harvesting ability, thus effectively improving the PEC performance of CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode.

To better understand how the catalyst improves the PEC water oxidation performance, the charge transfer and surface recombination kinetics of the modified electrode were analyzed by intensity modulated photocurrent spectroscopy (IMPS). The alternating current variation of light intensity is superimposed on the electrode illumination at a constant applied potential, and the periodic photocurrent response of the system is considered as a function of the modulation frequency. The semicircle of the first quadrant is related to the competition of interface minority carrier transfer and electron-hole recombination at the interface. The observed semicircles in the fourth quadrant provide information about the charge transfer and relaxation combinations within the photoanode. Thus, the frequency of the maximum imaginary part corresponds to the rate constants of charge transfer (k trans) and recombination (k rec) (k trans + k rec = 2πf max). In addition, the ratio of k trans/(k trans + k rec) can be derived from the low-frequency intercept and high-frequency intercept of the real axis (imaginary current = 0), so the key parameters k rec and k trans can be easily obtained [83]. Fig. 5 a exhibits the typical IMPS responses of CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode at different applied potentials. In addition, IMPS responses of BiVO4, CQDs/BiVO4 and Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanodes at 0.6 V vs. RHE are shown in Fig. 5b. The charge transfer rates of the difference electrodes were calculated from the IMPS response values and the corresponding formula (Fig. 5c). Using the bare BiVO4 electrode as a reference, we found that the k trans curve of CQDs/BiVO4 was very similar to that of Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 in the whole voltage range, while CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 exhibited a highest k trans value. The trend of k trans value indicates that CQDs and Ni(OH)2 on the electrode surface played an active role as a water oxidation catalyst. Fig. 5d shows the surface recombination rate constants of all electrodes at different potentials. The k rec behavior of Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 is close to CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 in the potentials range of 0.4–0.9 V vs. RHE. In contrast, the k rec value of CQDs/BiVO4 is higher, but tends to decrease with increase voltage. The lower k rec of Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 may be due to passivation of the Ni(OH)2 on the BiVO4 surface, which effectively inhibits the recombination of surface holes [84]. Furthermore, the k rec value of CQDs/BiVO4 is reduced, possibly due to the formation of heterojunction, which causes the bands on the surface of BiVO4 to bend, avoiding the internal interband recombination and promoting the hole injection [85]. Therefore, the CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 has a higher k trans values and lower k rec values, suggesting that the loading of CQDs and Ni(OH)2 can effectively reduce the charge recombination on the electrode surface, and the joint effect of co-modification can improve the charge transfer efficiency to a large extent.

The stability of the photoanode is also an important issue. The long-term stability test of the four sample photoanodes (BiVO4,CQDs/BiVO4,Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 and CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4) were conducted under illumination (Fig. 6 ). It is worth noting that the photocurrent slightly increases at the beginning of the experiment, which may be attributed to the infrared thermal heating of the electrolyte. All electrodes showed a rapid decrease in photocurrent at the beginning of the test, but with the increase of time, the photocurrent of CQDs/BiVO4 and Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 dropped faster, while CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 exhibited the highest photocurrent density and less decay (∼80% retention) in the 4 h test. In addition, the LSV curves of CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 showed a slight decrease in photocurrent density compared with the initial one after 4 h of testing (Fig. S16). At the same time, there was no obvious ultraviolet absorption peak in the electrolyte solution after 4 h of testing (Fig. S17). These results indicated that CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 exhibited excellent long-term stability in PEC water oxidation. The SEM images of the CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode showed no significant changes on the electrode surface after the stability test (Fig. S18). Comparing the XPS spectra before and after the stability test in Fig. S19, the change of the O 1s peak is mainly due to the fact that the binding energy of O is related to lattice oxygen, oxygen lattice hydroxyl, and adsorbed oxygen species. However, Bi 4f, V 2p, and Co 2p XPS spectrum has not changed, which also verify that the Ni(OH)2 catalyst is relatively stable. The decrease in photocurrent density may be due to the photocorrosion of BiVO4 and the desorption a small amount of catalyst.

In summary, we designed and constructed BiVO4 electrode modified after the interaction of CQDs and Ni(OH)2. When the two catalysts are loaded on the electrode, a partial way of two-step two-electron reaction is established which is more favorable in kinetics. The joint catalysis between CQDs and Ni(OH)2 significantly promoted the PEC water oxidation activity of BiVO4 electrode, which showed obvious cathode initial potential shift and enhanced photocurrent density. The CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 photoanode shows a water oxidation onset potential of about 0.2 V vs. RHE, and the photocurrent density is 3.05 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE, which is about 2.5 times that of the unmodified BiVO4 electrode. Moreover, CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 has the highest ABPE (0.90%) and IPCE (47%), and the hole injection efficiency is about 65%. Compared with BiVO4 electrode, CQDs + Ni(OH)2/BiVO4 showed higher Ktrans and lower Krec. Moreover, the construction of the co-modified catalyst can effectively improve the PEC properties of the photoanode, which provides important insights for the design and synthesis of efficient photoanodes for solar-driven water splitting.
